medical_history.history_of_present_illness: 2-year history of right lower limb numbness, gradually developed upper limb weakness with mild dysphoria and dysuria in the preceding month

physical_examination.specialty_examination: bilateral upper limb weakness (strength, 4/5), bilateral thenar eminence atrophy, and diminished superficial sensation in the lower limbs

laboratory_examinations.findings: World Health Organization grade II ependymoma. Microscopically, the small, elongated, round nuclear tumor cells were distributed around blood vessels. Histologic analysis was positive for yes-associated protein 1. Some tumor cells expressed the epithelial membrane antigen. Other immunohistochemical tests, including Olig-2, L1CAM, NeuN, IDH, and MIB-1, were negative.

radiographic_examinations.findings: A huge intramedullary tumor involving 10 segments, obviously enhanced from the foramen magnum to T3 level. The patient also presented with long syringomyelia spanning to L1. The spinal cord is almost invisible in the sagittal position, and it is compressed ventrally in the transverse view.
radiographic_examinations.changes_over_time: Three-month follow-up magnetic resonance imaging scan showed no residual tumor or recurrence, and whole spinal syringomyelia disappeared completely.

other_tests.findings: Bilateral upper limb weakness (strength, 4/5), bilateral thenar eminence atrophy, and diminished superficial sensation in the lower limbs.
other_tests.changes_over_time: At 6-month follow-up, the patient had full strength in all extremities and her numbness resolved.

diagnosis.final_diagnosis: World Health Organization grade II ependymoma
diagnosis.diagnostic_reasoning: The final diagnosis was confirmed through histologic analysis, which showed small, elongated, round nuclear tumor cells distributed around blood vessels. Immunohistochemical tests were positive for yes-associated protein 1 and epithelial membrane antigen, supporting the diagnosis of grade II ependymoma.
